topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box sat on the entryway table, a stark, incongruous splash of color against the muted beige of the hallway. Inside, a cascade of deep crimson roses lay nestled in cellophane, their petals impossibly perfect. No note. Just the flowers. I approached it slowly, my hand hovering over the cardboard. Every shadow in the apartment seemed to shift, elongating into something watchful. I checked the peephole, the cheap plastic fogging slightly with my breath. Who would leave something like this? It felt staged. A deliberate placement, an invitation for something unseen to observe. I picked up a single stem, the velvet texture cool beneath my fingertips, and immediately tucked it deep into my pocket, my knuckles white. I didn't want to look at the box again.

[story 2]

A sudden, sharp jolt of adrenaline spiked through my chest when I noticed the arrangement on my doorstep. Bright yellow lilies, aggressively cheerful, draped over the welcome mat. There was no inscription, no signature, nothing to anchor the gesture. My gaze darted around the living room, cataloging every dark corner, every reflection in the darkened television screen. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat I couldn't silence. I backed away from the door, pressing my spine against the cool wood. It wasn't a friendly gesture; it felt like a marker. Someone was watching the exchange, waiting for a reaction, waiting for me to make the wrong move. I snatched the flowers, stuffing them quickly into my bag, needing distance, needing to erase the evidence of this intrusion.

[story 3]

I found the bouquet resting on the kitchen counter, perfectly arranged beside the fruit bowl. White tulips, pristine and unnervingly still. The silence of the apartment amplified the slight rustle of the paper wrapping. I circled the counter twice, my movements jerky, assessing the placement of the shadows cast by the afternoon sun. Why leave it exposed? Why not slip it under the door? It was too obvious, too brazen. A hand slipped into my pocket, my fingers brushing against the stems. A cold tremor ran up my arm, a deep, unsettling vibration. I pulled my hand back as if burned, suddenly convinced that the flowers weren't a gift, but a signal, a silent declaration that I was being cataloged. I needed to hide them immediately, somewhere inaccessible.
